vitals.entry_fee: EUR 550-650 for the 5-stage entry, excluding accommodation, travel, and meals outside the event
vitals.feed_zones: Feed stops and meals on each of the 5 stages
vitals.cutoff_time: No overall time limit; only the timed uphill Crono per stage counts toward classification, ridden as an escorted convoy otherwise

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 74.

course_description.character: A five-day amateur road-cycling tour based around Bolzano that combines controlled-speed group riding with one traffic-free timed uphill Crono per stage — only those climbing segments count toward the daily and overall classification, so it rides more like a competitive cycling holiday than a conventional stage race. The 2026 route covered 345.8 km and 9,524 m across five stages of very different character, from a short 20.5 km transfer stage to the 107 km Sellaronda stage crossing Passo Sella, Pordoi, Campolongo and Gardena. Riders regroup around feed stops and cover the untimed portions in an escorted convoy, trading full-distance racing for guaranteed access to famous Dolomite passes.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Sellaronda stage (107 km, 3,102 m) — the event's biggest day, crossing four Giro d'Italia-storied Dolomite passes (Sella, Pordoi, Campolongo, Gardena) in one loop, with the timed Colfosco-Passo Gardena climb (6.3 km/439 m) as the classified test.
suffering_zones: Passo Nigra Crono (Stage 1) — 6.9 km/542 m with ramps to 13%, the event's opening timed test
Sellaronda (Stage 2) — Passo Sella, Pordoi, Campolongo and Gardena in one 107 km/3,102 m day, the queen stage by scale
Passo Rolle/San Pellegrino Crono (Stage 4) — 13.4 km/550 m timed to Passo Rolle, followed later by roughly 760 m more to Passo San Pellegrino
Terlano-Frassineto Crono (Stage 5) — 9 km/789 m closing climb, nearly 8.8% average, the event's final classified test

logistics.lodging_strategy: Bolzano base with hotels; bike-friendly options in South Tyrol; multi-day event allows central stay. Stage 2 starts at Ponte Gardena and Stage 4 at Predazzo, not Bolzano — an organizer bus between Bolzano and Predazzo costs an additional EUR 30.

history.origin_story: Organized by a Bolzano-based association founded more than 45 years ago; 2026 marked the 49th edition, one of the longest-running amateur multi-day road tours in the Dolomites.

biased_opinion.verdict: A Cycling Holiday Wearing a Race's Clothes
biased_opinion.summary: The 49th Giro delle Dolomiti (13-17 July 2026) is a genuinely unusual format: five days of controlled-pace group riding through South Tyrol's most famous passes, but only one traffic-free uphill Crono per stage actually counts for time. That means the overall classification is the sum of five short climbing efforts, not a continuous race — riders get the Sellaronda, Passo Rolle, and San Pellegrino under their wheels without needing to hold a race pace for 345.8 km. It's 49 editions deep (2020 cancelled due to COVID-19, resuming in 2021), runs on roughly 60 volunteers, and includes meals, mechanical support, luggage transfer, and massage — real infrastructure, not just a start list. But it isn't cheap (EUR 550-650 for the entry alone) and it isn't hub-and-spoke: stage starts shift between Bolzano, Ponte Gardena, and Predazzo, adding logistics most single-town gran fondos don't have.
strengths: 49 editions since 1977 (2020 was cancelled due to COVID-19, resuming in 2021) and genuine community infrastructure — roughly 60 volunteers, food stops, medical/mechanical support, luggage transfer
Access to iconic Dolomite passes (Sella, Pordoi, Campolongo, Gardena, Rolle, San Pellegrino) without needing to hold race pace across the untimed sections
A real international, sociable atmosphere distinct from a full-gas competitive stage race
Five varied stages spanning 20.5 to 107 km keep any single day from being unmanageable
weaknesses: Not a true stage race — the 'GC' is only the sum of five short timed Cronos, which will disappoint riders expecting continuous racing over 345.8 km
Expensive for a week: EUR 550-650 for the entry alone, before accommodation, travel, and meals outside the event
Logistics are not centralized in Bolzano — Stage 2 starts at Ponte Gardena and Stage 4 at Predazzo, with a paid EUR 30 bus transfer between Bolzano and Predazzo
Field is capped at 400 per stage and past that, priority goes to riders from Belluno/Trento/Bolzano and 10+-time returnees — a real barrier for first-time international entrants

Mid-July heat is a documented issue; past participants have described the event in terms of 'heat battles,' and a 400-rider controlled convoy mixing ability levels has produced reports of stressful braking and erratic descending
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you want the Dolomites' signature passes with organized support and don't need a continuous race clock, this delivers a well-run week. If you want a true multi-day stage race decided over full distance, the timed-Crono format will feel like a compromise.

final_verdict.one_liner: Historic 5-day Dolomites road epic blending social rides with brutal timed climbs.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want a supported week accessing the Sellaronda, Passo Rolle, and San Pellegrino without racing full distance. Skip it if you want a continuous, fully timed stage race — only short uphill Cronos count here.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a genuine timed multi-stage road race in the Dolomites region, look at shorter single-day granfondos on the same passes (e.g., Maratona dles Dolomites for a one-day timed event over similar terrain).

videos.title: Giro delle Dolomiti 2018 - 2° Stage "Obereggen" powered by Potenza
videos.description: Right after Bolzano we start climbing through the Val d’Ega. It’s a beautiful, however sometimes claustrophobic valley which takes us to Rauth where the timed stretch starts. The climb so far was gentle but it’s now that the tough part starts. For about 3km we climb at more then 12% until we see on our right the basecamp of the skislopes. Then it starts to flatten until the peak. Once passed over, we need to climb the Lavaze still before going back to Bolzano via the Val d’Ega at about lunchtime.
videos.curation_reason: Official course preview with specific terrain details including gradient percentages and key climbing sections.

videos.title: 43° Giro delle Dolomiti - trailer
videos.description: Nato più di 40 anni fa, il Giro delle Dolomiti è diventato probabilmente la più bella ed internazionale settimana (oppure i giorni a tua scelta) per i ciclisti amatoriali amanti della bici da corsa. Internazionale, unica, entusiasmante combina gara e velocità controllata in un’unica tappa, partendo sempre da Bolzano. A Bolzano in Alto Adige e nelle Dolomiti siamo di casa.

videos.title: 45° Giro delle Dolomiti trailer - 2022
videos.description: Welcome to the 45° edition of Giro delle Dolomiti from 24th – 30th July 2022. The oldest weeklong bicycle event for Dolomites lovers will take you through South Tyrol: the city of Bolzano, up to Renon, Passo delle Erbe, in Val Venosta and Val Sarentino, trough Trentino: in the Lake Valley and the city of Trento. Even more amazing and challenging!

rider_intel.search_text: Based on available rider accounts from the Giro delle Dolomiti, the primary challenge mentioned is extreme heat conditions during the race. Riders emphasize the significant impact of high temperatures on this 298-mile course through South Tyrol's mountainous terrain. The repeated emphasis on heat suggests this is a major factor riders should prepare for when tackling this challenging Italian gravel race. Limited transcript content available for this event.

eligibility.notes: 49th edition completed as scheduled 13-17 July 2026; organizer reported winners and posted post-event coverage. Not a continuous stage race — classification is the sum of one timed uphill Crono per stage; vitals corrected from a single-day 479.6km/9000m guess to the actual 5-stage total of 345.8km/9,524m.
